coredata.dc:title: A triple-channel sensor array utilizing fluorescent carbon dots for simultaneous discrimination and detection of multiple fluoroquinolones
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Xinghang
Zhong, Yujia
Chen, Anli
Bao, Tongyan
Deng, Qunfen
Zhang, Yu
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: The presence of fluoroquinolones (FQs) residues in food and the environment has prompted concerns regarding food safety and public health. Consequently, it is of great significance to analyze the types and levels of FQs present. However, the majority of studies have concentrated on the specific detection of individual FQs, with a notable absence of high-throughput and rapid analysis methods for the simultaneous detection of multiple FQs that may coexist in food and the environment. Hereon, a triple-channel sensor array was successfully constructed utilizing fluorescent carbon dots (TA-CDs), with the assistance of Cu2+ and Fe3+, for the qualitative discrimination and quantitative detection of eight types of FQs. The sensor array can distinguish between different concentrations of FQs and various mixtures of FQs, as well as 100 % accuracy in the discrimination of unknown samples. Impressively, the sensor platform can quantitatively detect FQs in animal-derived foods, such as honey, milk, eggs, and pork, as well as in water samples. This research has the potential to be extended to other analytes with similar chemical structures or properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor array
Carbon dots
Fluoroquinolones
Principal components analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication principle of the sensor array
Optimization of the detection conditions for the sensor system

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Special thanks to Southwest University Analytical Testing Center for providing technical support on the Steady State and Transient State Fluorescence Spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) and Multifunctional X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS SUPRA+010303, Shimadzu, Japan). Special thanks to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for help with the TEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Images of TA-CDs: (A) TEM, with the HR-TEM inset. (B) Particle size distribution histogram. (C) FTIR spectra. (D) XPS full spectra. (E) Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra, excitation (Ex) spectra, and emission (Em) spectra, with the TA-CDs solution under daylight and UV lamp photographed images in the inset. (F) 3D fluorescence spectra under different excitation (300–450 nm).
Fluorescence spectra of S1, S1-FQs, S2, S2-FQs, S3, and S3-FQs; the FQs (50 μM) are in order (A) ENO; (B) OFL; (C) PEF; (D) NOR; (E) CIP; (F) ENR; (G) GAT; and (H) SPA.

Results of the triple-channel sensor array distinguishing eight FQs (50 μM). (A) Fluorescence response fingerprint spectrogram. (B) Fluorescence response thermogram. (C) PCA score plot and (D) HCA plot.

PCA score plots for equimolar mixtures of SPA and seven other FQs, with total mixture concentrations of 50 μM (A) and 100 μM (B). (C) The PCA score plot for mixtures of SPA and CIP with unequal molar ratios, with total mixture concentrations of 50 μM. PCA score plots for three groups of blind selection experiments of unknown FQs at concentrations of (D) 25 μM; (E) 75 μM; and (F) 200 μM.

(A) PCA score plot of the sensor array discriminating different concentrations of ENO. (B) The working curve of a concentration gradient of ENO (5–150 μM) versus their corresponding PC1 values. (C) The fluorescence response spectra of ENO with the concentration gradient ranging from 0 to 400 μM in sensor element S1. (D) Relationship of (F–F
0 )/F
0 values in sensor element S1 versus concentration gradient ENO (0–400 μM).
(A) The synthesis process of TA-CDs. (B) The schematic diagram of the triple-channel sensor array for simultaneous discrimination and detection of eight types of FQs.
Analytical results of CIP and ENR in real samples (n = 5).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND: Not detected; RSD: Relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A triple-channel sensor array utilizing fluorescent carbon dots for simultaneous discrimination and detection of multiple fluoroquinolones

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of fluoroquinolones (FQs) residues in food and the environment has prompted concerns regarding food safety and public health. Consequently, it is of great significance to analyze the types and levels of FQs present. However, the majority of studies have concentrated on the specific detection of individual FQs, with a notable absence of high-throughput and rapid analysis methods for the simultaneous detection of multiple FQs that may coexist in food and the environment. Hereon, a triple-channel sensor array was successfully constructed utilizing fluorescent carbon dots (TA-CDs), with the assistance of Cu2+ and Fe3+, for the qualitative discrimination and quantitative detection of eight types of FQs. The sensor array can distinguish between different concentrations of FQs and various mixtures of FQs, as well as 100 % accuracy in the discrimination of unknown samples. Impressively, the sensor platform can quantitatively detect FQs in animal-derived foods, such as honey, milk, eggs, and pork, as well as in water samples. This research has the potential to be extended to other analytes with similar chemical structures or properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoroquinolones (FQs), a class of synthetic antibiotics, are commonly utilized for the prevention and treatment of various bacterial infections [1,2]. They are also frequently supplemented to animal feed in husbandry, poultry, or the aquaculture industry to prevent disease and promote animal growth due to their broad-spectrum efficacy, high sterilization effectiveness, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness [3,4]. However, the prolonged and widespread use of antibiotics, as well as inappropriate handling, can lead to excessive residues of FQs [5]. These residues may accumulate in the human body through animal-derived foods such as meat, poultry, eggs, aquatic products, and dairy products [6,7]. This accumulation can cause risk factors like allergic reactions and weakened immunity that threaten human health [8]. Therefore, developing a straightforward and efficient analysis strategy for accurately detecting and evaluating antibiotic residues in animal-derived foods, especially for simultaneous differentiation and rapid detection of multiple antibiotics, is of great significance and value [9].

Various methods have been applied to analyze and detect FQs, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [11], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [12], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [13,14], immunoassays [15], electrochemical analysis [16], and fluorescence analysis [17,18]. Most of these excellent studies focus on the analysis of a single FQs, however, in practical testing environments, it is common to encounter numerous different FQs analytes coexisting, and their high similarity in characteristics makes it challenging to achieve selective analysis of specific antibiotics. Fortunately, sensor arrays provide an effective idea for the simultaneous detection and discrimination of multiple compounds with similar structures or properties [19].

Researchers have developed sensor arrays, also known as chemical tongues/noses, inspired by the gustatory and olfactory systems of mammals [20,21]. Unlike conventional sensor systems that rely on a lock-and-key structure, sensor arrays utilize diverse interactions between various sensor units and analytes to generate a distinctive fingerprint response pattern [22,23]. The pattern is then statistically analyzed to enable pattern recognition and detection of multiple targets. Fluorescence signal-based sensor arrays, either through fluorescence quenching or enhancement, have become increasingly popular and widely applied due to their benefits of low cost, high accuracy, simple operation, and rapid response. Up to now, numerous sensor arrays rooted in semiconductor quantum dots [24], metal nanoclusters [25,26], carbon dots [27,28], and other fluorescent nanomaterials have been extensively researched. In particular, carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as ideal sensor array elements owing to their remarkable properties such as stable and tunable optical features, abundant surface functional groups, favorable biocompatibility and water-solubility, and a broad variety of raw materials [27,28]. Multichannel fluorescent sensor arrays created with CDs have been applied to analyze and identify heavy metal ions [29], phosphate anions [30], organic acids [31], thiols [19,32], proteins [27,33], tetracycline antibiotics [28,34], and a host of other comparable targets. However, there have been no reported fluorescence sensor arrays for the simultaneous detection of multiple FQs.

In this work, the nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (TA-CDs) were synthesized using tartaric acid and thiourea through a one-step solventless method (Scheme 1 A). TA-CDs possess abundant surface functional groups that enable them to interact with eight FQs simultaneously. However, it is challenging to discriminate multiple FQs simultaneously based on the response of a single sensor element. To address this issue, a sensor array was constructed using TA-CDs with the assistance of Cu2+ and Fe3+ to simultaneously discriminate and analyze eight FQs. Scheme 1B illustrates that the sensor array consists of three sensor elements, in order S1: TA-CDs, S2: TA-CDs@Cu2+, and S3: TA-CDs@Fe3+. Interestingly, all eight FQs (Table S1): enrofloxacin (ENO), ofloxacin (OFL), pefloxacin (PEF), norfloxacin (NOR), ciprofloxacin (CIP), enrofloxacin (ENR), gatifloxacin (GAT) and sparfloxacin (SPA) were found to interact with three sensor elements to produce different degrees of fluorescence response including fluorescence recovery or further fluorescence quenching. The respective unique fingerprint of each type of FQs and its corresponding thermogram can be obtained according to the rate of change of fluorescence intensity, (F–F
0 )/F
0 . Furthermore, principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) are performed on the numerical matrix of the response pattern. The results are presented in the form of a scatter diagram and a tree diagram, respectively, allowing for the discrimination and classification of various FQs. In addition, the sensor system was used to quantify levels of FQs in four animal-derived food products and two water samples, and the recovery rates of two randomly selected FQs (CIP, ENR) in the food and water samples were satisfactory.

1.50 g of tartaric acid and 1.52 g of thiourea were mixed homogeneously through a vortex mixer and then transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave, which was then heated in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling the product to room temperature, 15 mL of ultrapure water was added and sonicated for 10 min to ensure complete dissolution. Next, the large insoluble particles were removed by repeating centrifugation at 8000 r·min- 1 for 10 min twice. The supernatant obtained after centrifugation was eluted with acetone multiple times until it was no longer fluorescent [35,36]. Finally, the resulting oily precipitate was freeze-dried to collect the solid powder of carbon dots, which were labeled as TA-CDs.

Fabrication of three sensor elements. Three parts of 0.50 mL TA-CDs solution (6.0 mg mL- 1) were mixed with three parts of 0.50 mL Britton-Robinson buffer solution (pH = 7.0) in three identical colorimetric tubes recorded S1, S2, and S3, respectively. To tube S2, 0.05 mL of 20 mM Cu2+ was added, and to tube S3, 0.10 mL of 20 mM Fe3+ was added. Then the solution in the three colorimetric tubes was diluted with ultrapure water to a volume of 2.0 mL and incubated at room temperature for 2 min. The initial fluorescence intensity F
0 was measured for channels S1, S2, and S3 using an excitation (Ex) wavelength of 340 nm.

Discrimination and detection of FQs. Appropriate amounts of FQs were added to the three sensor elements, S1, S2, and S3, ensuring the final mixed solution volume remained at 2.0 mL. The fluorescence intensity F of each sensor unit was tested at λ
ex = 340 nm after incubation for 2 min at room temperature following the addition of FQs. The construction of a multi-dimensional sensor array can be achieved by utilizing the various response signal parameters related to changes in fluorescence intensity before and after adding each sensor unit to FQs.

Three groups of blind selection experiments were conducted to evaluate the discrimination accuracy of the sensor array. The blind selection samples were prepared and numbered by another member of the laboratory who randomly selected several of the eight FQs. Specifically, concentrations of 25 μM were labeled as numbers 1 to 5, concentrations of 75 μM were labeled as numbers 6 to 10, and concentrations of 200 μM were labeled as numbers 11 to 15. Subsequently, the aforementioned blind samples (totaling fifteen) underwent testing with our designed sensor array. The resulting experimental data was then compared with the standard data to determine the identity of the unknown samples based on which FQs they contained.

To assess the selectivity and interference resistance of the sensor array, a series of interfering substances were added to the array. These included glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose, lysine, tryptophan, methionine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, threonine, valine, KI, CaCl2, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, Na2HPO4, MgSO4, Zn(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, and H3BO3, which may exist in real samples. Additionally, the sensor array's performance was tested in three different environments: tap water, lake water, and milk, to further evaluate its anti-interference capability. The study utilized tap water from the laboratory, lake water from Chongde Lake of Southwest University, and milk purchased from Yonghui Supermarket in Chongqing. The pretreatment of the aforementioned samples was detailed in the Supplementary Material. The experimental conditions and analytical methods described in Section 2.2 remained unchanged for the tests. The only alteration made was the substitution of ultrapure water with tap water, lake water, or milk.

Two FQs (CIP, ENR) were randomly selected and added to food samples (honey, milk, eggs, and pork) and water samples (tap water and lake water) for standard recovery experiments to further evaluate the practical application of the sensor system. The food samples purchased from Yonghui supermarket in Chongqing were pretreated following the instructions provided in the Supplementary Material. The processed samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use. To conduct the standard recovery experiments, the prepared FQs standard solution was artificially added to the actual samples. The resulting fluorescence intensity changes were recorded and used to calculate the recovery and relative standard deviation by applying the working curve.

As the central component of the sensor system, the structural, optical, and stability properties of the TA-CDs were characterized and analyzed initially. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the morphology and particle size of the TA-CDs microstructures. Fig. 1 A illustrates that the TA-CDs are primarily monodisperse quasi-spherical structures. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images show obvious lattice stripes with lattice spacings of 0.21 and 0.34 nm, corresponding to the intra-facial lattice spacing on the graphene (100) facet and the lattice spacing on the inter-layered (002) facet of graphene, respectively [37,38]. The map of particle size distribution, obtained from statistical analysis of 150 random TA-CDs nanoparticles (Fig. 1B), suggests that the particle size of TA-CDs is centrally distributed in the range of 3.0–8.0 nm, with an average particle size of 5.5 ± 0.5 nm. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy can provide information about the surface functional groups of TA-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1C, the characteristic peaks of O–H (3468 cm- 1), N–H (3138 cm- 1), C=O (1676 cm- 1), and C–O (1316 cm- 1) stretching vibrations confirm the presence of hydrophilic groups (–NH2, –OH, and –COOH) in TA-CDs. The absorption peaks near 2833 cm- 1, 1596 cm- 1, 1401 cm- 1, 1119 cm- 1, and 1070 cm- 1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the functional groups C–H, C=C/C=N, C–N, C–S, and C=S, respectively [39–41]. This suggests the presence of both nitrogen-sulfur-containing groups and conjugated aromatic structures in the TA-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is instrumental in measuring and analyzing the surface groups and elemental contents of TA-CDs. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) indicates that TA-CDs consisted of four elements, C (63.48 %), N (13.17 %), O (21.47 %), and S (1.88 %). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p are presented in Fig. S1, along with the corresponding analytical results. In conclusion, the XPS results further confirm the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur elements in TA-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1E, it is evident that TA-CDs exhibit the optimum emission peak located at 417 nm at the best excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Furthermore, the TA-CDs solution appears light yellow in daylight and emits blue fluorescence under the excitation of a 365 nm UV lamp. The relative fluorescence quantum yield of TA-CDs was calculated to be 14.64 %. The 3D fluorescence spectrogram in Fig. 1F displays the fluorescence emission of TA-CDs with typical excitation-dependent properties. Furthermore, the luminescence stability of TA-CDs was investigated, presented in Fig. S2, to ensure a dependable and effective fluorescence detection system for constructing the sensor array.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication principle of the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely recognized that the selection and combination of sensor elements are crucial in constructing a sensor array. Literature research has revealed that sensor arrays constructed based on CDs can be typically classified into two types of sensor systems: sensor arrays in the CDs-analyte direct response mode [28,31] and sensor arrays in the CDs-sensor medium-analyte indirect response mode [32,42]. In the latter case, the sensor medium is often a metal-ion medium capable of producing an “on-off-on” or “on-off” response pattern [30,42,43]. As shown in Fig. S3, Cu2+ and Fe3+ were chosen as the sensor medium for the construction of the sensor array based on TA-CDs due to their effective quenching of the fluorescence of the TA-CDs. A comparison of the UV–vis absorption spectra of TA-CDs before and after the addition of Cu2+/Fe3+ (Fig. S4A) revealed the emergence of new absorption peaks, indicating the possibility of the formation of ground state complexes, TA-CDs@Cu2+ and TA-CDs@Fe3+. Fig. S4B also demonstrates that the fluorescence lifetimes of TA-CDs before and after the addition of Cu2+ and Fe3+ remained essentially unchanged. This further corroborates that the cause of the quenching of TA-CDs by Cu2+ and Fe3+ may be attributed to static quenching.

To verify the feasibility of this design, eight FQs (ENO, OFL, PEF, NOR, CIP, ENR, GAT, SPA) at a concentration of 50 μM were sequentially introduced to the sensor array. As shown in Fig. 2 , it is evident that the addition of FQs to the three sensor elements resulted in varying degrees of fluorescence response changes, either fluorescence recovery or further fluorescence quenching, due to the different interaction forces between the FQs and the sensor elements. It is worth noting that discriminating multiple types of FQs only using a single sensor element presents a significant challenge. However, by combining the differential fluorescence response signals in three sensor elements, namely TA-CDs and FQs, TA-CDs@Cu2+ and FQs, and TA-CDs@Fe3+ and FQs, it becomes possible to detect eight types of FQs simultaneously. Furthermore, it is also feasible to establish an analysis mode for each type of FQs.

Fig. 3 A shows the distinct optical fingerprint spectrograms of each type of FQs in the three sensor elements, as determined by the fluorescence intensity rate of change (F–F
0 )/F
0 response signals. The fluorescence response pattern of each type of FQs can be observed more intuitively from the fluorescence response thermograms (Fig. 3B). To ensure the reliability and accuracy of the experimental data, we carried out five repeated experiments and generated a numerical matrix of 3 × 8 × 5 (three sensor elements × eight types of FQs × five repeated experiments). The numerical matrix samples underwent PCA analysis, from which a two-dimensional score plot was created using the first score PC1 as the X-axis and the second score PC2 as the Y-axis. The scatter plot in Fig. 3C displays the distribution of the eight FQs based on the PCA score results. The eight types of FQs are visually presented as eight independent clusters, confirming the successful discrimination and differentiation of each type by the sensor array. Additionally, the numerical matrices were also evaluated by HCA. Fig. 3D shows that each type of FQs is accurately categorized into distinct branches of the clustering dendrogram, providing further evidence of the exceptional recognition capabilities of the constructed triple-channel sensor array.

The possible mechanism for the distinctive response between sensor arrays and different FQs was investigated using ENO and ENR as examples. The ENO serves as a representative example of the further quenching of the fluorescent signal in the sensing elements S1/S2/S3, while the ENR represents a typical example of the fluorescent signal being enhanced (Figs. S5A, D, and G). As illustrated in Fig. S5B, the addition of ENO did not result in a notable alteration to the absorption spectrum of S1, indicating that ENO did not bind to S1 in the ground state and that no static quenching was present. Concurrently, there is an apparent overlap between the absorption peak of ENO and the excitation peak of S1, and the fluorescence lifetime of S1 remains largely unaltered following the addition of ENO (Fig. S5C), suggesting that the fluorescence quenching of S1 resulting from the addition of ENO can be attributed to the internal filtration effect (IFE). It is noteworthy that although the absorption peak of ENR overlaps with the excitation peak of S1, an enhancement of fluorescence was observed following the addition of ENR to S1. This phenomenon may be attributed to the absence of static or dynamic quenching between ENR and S1, which can be confirmed by the results of the absorption spectrum (Fig. S5B) and fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S5C), coupled with the strong fluorescence emission of ENR itself under 340 nm excitation. The differential discrimination signals of ENO/ENR by sensor elements S2 and S3 exhibit a similar variation trend to that of S1. However, the fluorescence signals are comparatively weaker due to the presence of Cu2+/Fe3+ in the sensor elements. Consequently, the interaction between Cu2+/Fe3+ and ENO/ENR was subjected to investigation. The results presented in Fig. S6 demonstrate that ENR is capable of binding to Cu2+/Fe3+ in the ground state, while ENO exhibits a significantly weaker affinity. It is reasonable to hypothesize that the addition of ENR competes with the Cu2+/Fe3+ bound to TA-CDs in S2/S3, thereby restoring the fluorescence of TA-CDs and resulting in an enhanced fluorescence signal in S2/S3. In contrast to ENR, the addition of ENO is more likely to preferentially interact with TA-CDs in S2/S3 (IFE) to further quench the fluorescence signal, rather than competing for Cu2+/Fe3+ binding to TA-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S5 E and H, the addition of ENR resulted in notable alterations to the absorption spectra of S2 and S3, further confirming the formation of ground-state complexes. The addition of ENO did not result in any significant alterations to the absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime of S2 and S3, which also supports the aforementioned conclusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection conditions for the sensor system
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve optimal sensor performance, the detection conditions were optimized by controlling the variables. The amount of Cu2+ and Fe3+ added to the sensor element was optimized initially. In Figs. S7A and B, different concentration gradients of Cu2+ or Fe3+ were added to the TA-CDs solution sequentially from low to high. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of TA-CDs reached a higher level and gradually stabilized after the addition of Cu2+ up to 250 μM and Fe3+ up to 500 μM in the TA-CDs@Mn + system. Based on these results, we investigated the ability of the sensor array to discriminate multiple FQs simultaneously while varying the addition concentrations of Cu2+ in the S2 sensor element (250 μM, 500 μM, and 750 μM) and Fe3+ in the S3 sensor element (500 μM, 750 μM, 1000 μM, and 1250 μM). Based on the PCA score plot (Fig. S8), the combination of sensor elements with 500 μM Cu2+ and 1000 μM Fe3+ added is the most appropriate for differentiation. Additionally, the incubation times required for each step in the sensor process were explored at room temperature. Fig. S9 demonstrates that the sensor system can quickly achieve response balance and maintain stability, whether metal ions were added to construct sensor elements or FQs were added to the culture. Furthermore, the discriminatory performance of the sensor array for multiple FQs was evaluated in pH environments ranging from 2 to 12. The results depicted in Fig. S10 illustrate that our designed triple-channel sensor array can effectively differentiate eight FQs in various pH environments, with optimal discriminative effect achieved in a neutral environment at pH = 7.

The applicability of the sensor array was evaluated by testing the discrimination of eight FQs at different concentrations (5 μM, 25 μM, 75 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, and 300 μM) under optimal detection conditions. As depicted in Fig. S11, there is a clear separation and no overlap between the data points of the eight types of FQs in the PCA score plots. This fully demonstrates the exceptional discrimination ability of the sensor array. Furthermore, as multiple analytes may coexist during practical analytical tests, the sensor array was utilized to discriminate mixed samples of FQs. The mixture samples included equimolar mixtures consisting of SPA and seven other FQs, as well as mixtures of SPA and CIP with unequal molar ratios (SPA/CIP = 100/0, 80/20, 60/40, 40/60, 20/80, 0/100, total concentration 50 μM). It is clearly observed from Fig. 4A, B, and C that the mixed FQs samples of any molar ratio can be effectively distinguished by the sensor array. This result further reveals that the sensor array has the potential to analyze complex samples.

Subsequently, blind selection experiments were conducted to assess the stability and applicability of the sensor array according to its accuracy in recognizing unknown samples. The results of accurately distinguishing the eight types of FQs at a certain concentration served as a standard reference. The fluorescence response data of the unknown samples were measured under the same experimental conditions and compared with the standard map to determine which of the eight types of FQs they belonged to. The results of blind selection recognition were presented in Fig. 4D, E, and F. The sensor array demonstrates 100 % discrimination accuracy for 15 unknown samples, indicating its reliability and accuracy in recognizing FQs. Additionally, we found that confirming the concentration values of unknown concentration FQs can be accomplished by creating a standard map library.

In practical applications, the sensor system must possess strong anti-interference capability as it is often carried out in more complex environments than those found in experimental conditions. To investigate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the sensor array, 21 types of interference substances were artificially added to the sensor array at a concentration 10 times higher than that of FQs, which may be present in the actual sample. As shown in Fig. S12A, the presence of interfering substances does not affect the sensor array's ability to distinguish FQs. A discrimination test was carried out on FQs by the sensor array in tap water, lake water, and milk environments to evaluate the anti-interference ability and practical application performance of the sensor system. Figs. S12B, C, and D illustrate that the sensor array can accurately recognize FQs even in complex environments. The above results confirm that the triple-channel sensor array has excellent anti-interference capability and performance in complex environments.

After qualitatively discriminating eight types of FQs, the study investigated whether the sensor array could quantitatively analyze these targets under optimal detection conditions. As an example, the fluorescence response to the concentration gradient of ENO was measured using the sensor array. The numerical matrix representing the response was processed for each concentration through PCA. The PCA score plot in Fig. 5 A clearly demonstrates that the sensor array effectively differentiated between different concentrations of ENO. Furthermore, Fig. 5B demonstrates a linear relationship between ENO concentrations ranging from 5 to 150 μM and their corresponding PC1 values. It is significant to note that the quantitative analysis of all eight types of FQs can also be achieved by measuring the fluorescence intensity change rate (F–F
0
)/F
0 in sensor element S1. The strong linear relationship between (F–F
0 )/F
0 values in sensor element S1 and the concentration gradient of ENO is clearly demonstrated in Fig. 5C and D. This relationship is also observed for the remaining seven FQs, as shown in Figs. S13–S19, with a summary of the results provided in Table S2. Our approach exhibits a wider linear range and superior linear regression constants (R2 > 0.99) compared to other methods for detecting FQs reported in the literature (Table S3).

To ensure the reproducibility and practicality of this method, CIP and ENR were randomly selected from eight types of FQs to carry out standard recovery experiments in tap water, lake water, and four types of food samples (milk, honey, eggs, and pork). According to the results shown in Table 1 , the recovery rates of CIP ranged from 95.40 % to 106.80 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.29 %–5.94 %, while the recovery rates of ENR ranged from 95.20 % to 104.56 % with RSD of 0.57 %–6.90 % across different environments. These findings further validate the feasibility of this work, indicating that the triple-channel sensor array can be effectively used for both qualitative discrimination and quantitative analysis of the selected FQs.

In summary, this study successfully developed a triple-channel sensor array based on TA-CDs, with the assistance of Cu2+ and Fe3+, for the qualitative discrimination and quantitative detection of eight types of FQs. The distinct fluorescence response signals from eight types of FQs in the three sensor elements were monitored to obtain a unique fingerprint recognition pattern. Upon further analyzing the numerical matrices of the response signals using PCA or HCA, the excellent recognition of eight types of FQs by the sensor array was presented very intuitively in the form of a two-dimensional score plot or a tree diagram. The sensor array demonstrates exceptional performance in discriminating various concentrations of FQs and FQs mixtures, as well as 100 % accuracy in the identification of unknown samples. Additionally, this array method exhibits rapid response time, superior anti-interference capability, and potential for the application of detecting multiple analytes in complex environments, such as tap water, lake water, or animal-derived food. This work provides an effective approach for simultaneous discrimination and detection of multiple analytes with similar chemical structures or characteristics.

Xinghang Yin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yujia Zhong: Methodology, Conceptualization. Anli Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis. Tongyan Bao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Qunfen Deng: Investigation, Data curation. Yu Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Rui Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Special thanks to Southwest University Analytical Testing Center for providing technical support on the Steady State and Transient State Fluorescence Spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) and Multifunctional X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS SUPRA+010303, Shimadzu, Japan). Special thanks to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for help with the TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126608.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of sustainable manganese ferrite modified biochar from vinasse for enhanced adsorption of fluoroquinolone antibiotics: effects and mechanisms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xiang, X. Yang, Z. Xu, W. Hu, Y. Zhou, Z. Wan, Y. Yang, Y. Wei, J. Yang, D.C.W. Tsang, Fabrication of sustainable manganese ferrite modified biochar from vinasse for enhanced adsorption of fluoroquinolone antibiotics: Effects and mechanisms, Science of The Total Environment, 709 (2020) 136079.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A direct DRS-FTIR probe for rapid detection and quantification of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in poultry egg-yolk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Kurrey, M. Mahilang, M. Kanti Deb, J. Nirmalkar, K. Shrivas, S. Pervez, M. Kumar Rai, J. Rai, A direct DRS-FTIR probe for rapid detection and quantification of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in poultry egg-yolk, Food Chemistry, 270 (2019) 459-466.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective extraction and enhanced-sensitivity detection of fluoroquinolones in swine body fluids by liquid chromatography-high resolution mass spectrometry: application in long-term monitoring in livestock

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Wang, S. Li, D. Chen, Y. Zhao, Y. Wu, K. Qi, Selective extraction and enhanced-sensitivity detection of fluoroquinolones in swine body fluids by liquid chromatography-high resolution mass spectrometry: Application in long-term monitoring in livestock, Food Chemistry, 341 (2021) 128269.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current analytical strategies for the determination of quinolone residues in milk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, Y. Luo, W. Li, X. Xu, B. Wang, X. Xu, D. Hussain, D. Chen, Current analytical strategies for the determination of quinolone residues in milk, Food Chemistry, 430 (2024) 137072.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a new dual electrochemical immunosensor for a rapid and sensitive detection of enrofloxacin in meat samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Aymard, H. Kanso, M.J. Serrano, R. Pagan, T. Noguer, G. Istamboulie, Development of a new dual electrochemical immunosensor for a rapid and sensitive detection of enrofloxacin in meat samples, Food Chemistry, 370 (2022) 131016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current advances in immunoassays for quinolones in food and environmental samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Pan, H. Yang, K. Wen, Y. Ke, J. Shen, Z. Wang, Current advances in immunoassays for quinolones in food and environmental samples, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry, 157 (2022) 116726.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of fluorescent sensor array and three-dimensional microfluidic paper based analytical device for specific identification and visual determination of antibiotics in food

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Li, G. Guo, H. Xing, S. Tang, H. Hu, L. Wang, X. Qian, D. Chen, Construction of fluorescent sensor array and three-dimensional microfluidic paper based analytical device for specific identification and visual determination of antibiotics in food, Food Chemistry, 429 (2023) 136947.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterial-based fluorescent biosensors for the detection of antibiotics in foodstuffs: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Singh, B. Thakur, S.K. Bhardwaj, M. Khatri, K.-H. Kim, N. Bhardwaj, Nanomaterial-based fluorescent biosensors for the detection of antibiotics in foodstuffs: A review, Food Chemistry, 426 (2023) 136657.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new semiconductor heterojunction SERS substrate for ultra-sensitive detection of antibiotic residues in egg

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, Y. Tang, W. Wang, D. Yu, L. Yang, X. Jiang, W. Song, B. Zhao, A new semiconductor heterojunction SERS substrate for ultra-sensitive detection of antibiotic residues in egg, Food Chemistry, 431 (2024) 137163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic Liquid-functionalized magnetic metal-organic framework nanocomposites for efficient extraction and sensitive detection of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in environmental water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Lu, M. Qin, C. Liu, J. Deng, G. Shi, T. Zhou, Ionic Liquid-functionalized magnetic metal-organic framework nanocomposites for efficient extraction and sensitive detection of fluoroquinolone antibiotics in environmental water, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 13 (2021) 5357-5367.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantification of fluoroquinolones in wastewaters by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Maia, P. Paiga, C. Delerue-Matos, P.M.L. Castro, M.E. Tiritan, Quantification of fluoroquinolones in wastewaters by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry, Environmental Pollution, 259 (2020) 113927.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pressure-assisted electrokinetic injection stacking for seven typical antibiotics in waters to achieve μg/L level analysis by capillary electrophoresis with UV detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. He, Z. Xu, J. Ren, Pressure-assisted electrokinetic injection stacking for seven typical antibiotics in waters to achieve μg/L level analysis by capillary electrophoresis with UV detection, Microchemical Journal, 146 (2019) 1295-1300.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic Fe3O4@COF@Ag SERS substrate combined with machine learning algorithms for detection of three quinolone antibiotics: ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin and levofloxacin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Yang, G. Chen, C. Ma, J. Gu, C. Zhu, L. Li, H. Gao, Magnetic Fe3O4@COF@Ag SERS substrate combined with machine learning algorithms for detection of three quinolone antibiotics: Ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin and levofloxacin, Talanta, 263 (2023) 124725.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of several quinolone antibiotic residues in water based on Ag-TiO2 SERS strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Wang, Q. Sang, M. Yang, J. Du, L. Yang, X. Jiang, X. Han, B. Zhao, Detection of several quinolone antibiotic residues in water based on Ag-TiO2 SERS strategy, Science of The Total Environment, 702 (2020) 134956.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoprism-based plasmonic ELISA for sensitive detection of fluoroquinolones

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yuan, Q. Xiong, G. Zhang, Z. Xiong, D. Liu, H. Duan, W. Lai, Silver nanoprism-based plasmonic ELISA for sensitive detection of fluoroquinolones, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, 8 (2020) 3667-3675.
K. Rudnicki, K. Sipa, M. Brycht, P. Borgul, S. Skrzypek, L. Poltorak, Electrochemical sensing of fluoroquinolone antibiotics, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry, 128 (2020) 115907.

Luo, Conversion of fluorescence signals into optical fingerprints realizing high-throughput discrimination of anionic sulfonate surfactants with similar structure based on a statistical strategy and luminescent metal-organic frameworks, Analytical Chemistry, 92 (2020) 7273-7281.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Jin, Y. Sun, N. Du, W.-T. Dou, Y. Tan, X.-P. He, N. Xu, C. Tan, Rapid identification of molecular subtyping of breast cancer cell lines using a multi-channel sensor array, Sensors & Diagnostics, 2 (2023) 837-841.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Pu, J. Ren, X. Qu, Recent progress in sensor arrays using nucleic acid as sensing elements, Coordination Chemistry Reviews, 456 (2022) 214379.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Aznar-Gadea, P.J. Rodriguez-Canto, S.A. Sanchez, J.P. Martinez-Pastor, R. Abargues, Luminescent CdSe quantum dot arrays for rapid sensing of explosive taggants, ACS Applied Nano Materials, 5 (2022) 6717-6725.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, R. Hu, Z.-Q. Fang, G. Shi, S. Zhang, M. Zhang, A multifunctional upconversion nanoparticles probe for Cu2+ sensing and pattern recognition of biothiols, Chinese Chemical Letters, 33 (2022) 3782-3786.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sun, Y. Lu, L. He, J. Pang, F. Yang, Y. Liu, Colorimetric sensor array based on gold nanoparticles: Design principles and recent advances, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry, 122 (2020) 115754.

Xu, Array-based sensing of amyloidogenic proteins and discrimination of cancer by using different oxidants doped carbon nanodots as fluorescent probes, Chemical Engineering Journal, 430 (2022) 132696.
